stem:
A 30-year-old G2P1 attends antenatal clinic at 28+0 weeks for review of her routine gestational diabetes screening. She had a previous baby weighing 4.3 kg born at 40+1 weeks. Her booking BMI was 31 kg per square metre. She has no family history of type 2 diabetes and her first pregnancy did not require diabetes screening because of a different antenatal provider.

She completed a 75 g two-hour oral glucose tolerance test yesterday after an overnight fast. She is asymptomatic and her foetus is active. There is no glycosuria on urine dipstick today and her blood pressure is normal. She has no symptoms of polyuria or polydipsia. Her medications are routine pregnancy multivitamin only and she does not smoke. Her booking HbA1c was 5.4 percent.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 78, blood pressure 122/76, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: OGTT fasting plasma glucose 5.4 mmol/L (target < 5.1), one hour 10.8 mmol/L (target < 10.0), two hour 8.6 mmol/L (target < 8.5)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure her that the oral glucose tolerance test is within normal range and review her at the routine 36 week appointment with no further action
options.rationale: All three OGTT timepoints exceed ADIPS thresholds. The diagnosis of gestational diabetes is made when any one of fasting >= 5.1, one hour >= 10.0, or two hour >= 8.5 mmol/L is met. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Repeat the oral glucose tolerance test in four weeks because results are only marginally elevated and might normalise spontaneously
options.rationale: Repeat testing is not part of the ADIPS pathway when threshold criteria are met. The diagnosis is made on a single abnormal value. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Commence insulin glargine 10 units at night and review with diabetes educator and dietitian once weekly until delivery and the postnatal review
options.rationale: First-line therapy for gestational diabetes is lifestyle intervention and home blood glucose monitoring. Insulin is added only when glycaemic targets are not met after 1 to 2 weeks of diet and exercise. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Diagnose GDM, refer to diabetes educator and dietitian, start home blood glucose monitoring with fasting target under 5.1 and two hour postprandial under 6.7 mmol/L
options.rationale: All three OGTT values exceed ADIPS criteria, confirming GDM. First-line management is education, dietary modification, exercise, and home glucose monitoring with fasting target less than 5.1 and two-hour postprandial less than 6.7 mmol/L; pharmacotherapy is added if targets not met. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Diagnose GDM and commence oral metformin 500 mg twice daily as first-line pharmacotherapy with dietitian review at the next clinic visit
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is escalated only when lifestyle measures fail to achieve targets over 1 to 2 weeks. Metformin remains a second-line option after insulin in Australian practice. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

explanation.summary: ADIPS diagnostic criteria for GDM on the 75 g 2-hour OGTT are fasting plasma glucose 5.1 or more, one hour 10.0 or more, or two hour 8.5 or more mmol/L. Any one value confirms the diagnosis. First-line management is dietary advice, exercise, and home blood glucose monitoring with targets fasting under 5.1 and two-hour postprandial under 6.7 mmol/L. Insulin is the preferred first-line pharmacotherapy when targets are not met; metformin is second line in Australian practice.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds (any one): fasting >= 5.1, 1-hour >= 10.0, 2-hour >= 8.5 mmol/L
First-line: lifestyle, dietitian, home blood glucose monitoring
Targets: fasting < 5.1, two hour postprandial < 6.7 mmol/L
Pharmacotherapy if targets not met after 1-2 weeks lifestyle
Postpartum OGTT at 6-12 weeks to detect persistent dysglycaemia
explanation.pearls: ADIPS uses fasting cut-off 5.1 mmol/L (lower than non-pregnant 6.1) reflecting the heightened risk of large-for-gestational-age and neonatal hypoglycaemia even at modest fasting elevations.

stem:
A 24-year-old G1P0 at 38+2 weeks is in established labour in the delivery suite. The registrar performs an artificial rupture of membranes for slow progress. Cervix is 6 cm dilated, head is high at station minus one, contractions are three in ten and strong. Immediately after the artificial rupture of membranes the midwife observes a 6 cm length of umbilical cord prolapsing through the cervix into the vagina.

Foetal cardiotocograph immediately falls from a baseline of 140 to 80 with a flat trace. The mother is positioned supine on the bed. The emergency caesarean section is called overhead and the obstetric registrar arrives at the bedside within one minute. The anaesthetic team is paged and a theatre is being prepared on the floor below. The mother is calm but anxious and has had no opioid analgesia in the last six hours.

data_table.Vital signs: maternal pulse rate 96, blood pressure 118/72, respiratory rate 20, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees, foetal heart rate 80

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Replace the cord into the vagina manually with continuous foetal monitoring and proceed to ventouse-assisted vaginal delivery in the delivery suite
options.rationale: Manual cord replacement is not recommended because it does not reliably restore foetal circulation and ventouse is not feasible at 6 cm dilation. The head is also too high for instrumental delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Position the mother left lateral, administer terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneously for tocolysis, and observe over five minutes
options.rationale: Tocolysis may be considered as an adjunct to relieve contractions while preparing for caesarean, but it is not sufficient definitive management when the cord is prolapsed and foetal bradycardia is present. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Elevate the presenting part with the examining hand, adopt knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, and transfer immediately to theatre for emergency caesarean section
options.rationale: Manual elevation of the presenting part to relieve cord compression plus a knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position and immediate transfer to theatre for category-one caesarean section is the recommended management. Decision-to-delivery interval target is under 30 minutes. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Apply suprapubic pressure with simultaneous fundal pressure and instruct the mother to push with each contraction for rapid vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Pushing increases cord compression and worsens foetal asphyxia. Vaginal delivery is not appropriate at 6 cm dilation with a high head. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Insert urinary catheter and instill 500 mL of saline to elevate the presenting part, then proceed to instrumental delivery in delivery suite
options.rationale: Bladder filling can be used as a bridging measure when caesarean delivery is delayed (for example in transit), but it does not replace manual elevation and immediate theatre transfer. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is a category-one obstetric emergency. Immediate management is manual elevation of the presenting part to relieve cord compression, knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, and transfer to theatre for emergency caesarean section under general anaesthesia, targeting a decision-to-delivery interval under 30 minutes. Tocolysis (terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneous) and bladder filling are bridging adjuncts when transfer is delayed. The cord should be kept warm and moist and not handled excessively.
key_points: Immediate manual elevation of the presenting part
Knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position
Category-one caesarean: decision-to-delivery under 30 minutes
Tocolysis with terbutaline and bladder filling are bridging adjuncts
Keep cord warm and moist, avoid excessive handling
explanation.pearls: Bladder filling with 500 mL of saline through a urinary catheter is a bridging manoeuvre for cord prolapse during transfer (e.g. retrieval to a tertiary centre) and does not replace continued manual elevation.

subtopic_detail: Severe HELLP delivery timing with steroid uncertainty
stem:
A 29-year-old G1P0 at 33+0 weeks is transferred to your tertiary delivery suite from a regional hospital with severe pre-eclampsia. She has had epigastric pain and nausea for 12 hours and her blood pressure has risen to 175/115 despite oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily. She has had no seizures. Foetal movements remain normal.

On examination she has right upper quadrant tenderness without peritonism and is alert (GCS 15) with hyperreflexia. Cardiotocograph is reactive, baseline 140, normal variability, no decelerations. Foetal estimated weight on ultrasound today is 1900 g (50th centile). Cervix is unfavourable on examination (Bishop score 2). Tertiary neonatal intensive care is available. Magnesium sulfate has been loaded and a labetalol infusion is running. No corticosteroid has yet been administered.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 108, blood pressure 168/108 on labetalol infusion, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees, foetal heart rate 144 reactive
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 96 g/L (115-145, was 122 yesterday), platelets 78 x10^9/L (150-400, was 140 yesterday), ALT 312 U/L (was 80 yesterday), LDH 580 U/L (110-230), haptoglobin 0.1 g/L (0.3-2.0), peripheral blood film schistocytes present, urine protein:creatinine ratio 380 mg/mmol, creatinine 92 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue magnesium sulfate and labetalol infusion and proceed to induction of labour with prostaglandin pessary targeting vaginal delivery within 24 hours
options.rationale: Induction with an unfavourable cervix (Bishop 2) and severe progressive HELLP is unsafe; the time required for induction is incompatible with the rate of maternal decline. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue magnesium sulfate and labetalol infusion plus give antenatal betamethasone and expectant management for 48 hours to complete the steroid course
options.rationale: Expectant management for the full 48 hour steroid window is contraindicated when HELLP is progressing rapidly (platelets falling 140 to 78, ALT rising 80 to 312 within 24 hours). Maternal compromise outweighs the benefit of a second betamethasone dose. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue magnesium and labetalol infusion plus give antenatal betamethasone and intravenous dexamethasone 10 mg twice daily as treatment for HELLP itself
options.rationale: High-dose dexamethasone does not improve maternal or perinatal outcomes in HELLP (Cochrane 2010) and is not a substitute for delivery. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue magnesium and labetalol infusion, transfuse platelets to greater than 100, and book elective caesarean section in twelve hours after platelet recovery
options.rationale: Routine prophylactic platelet transfusion in HELLP is not indicated unless platelets are below 20 or there is bleeding or planned regional anaesthesia at very low counts. Delaying delivery for twelve hours allows further deterioration. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue magnesium and labetalol infusion, give a first dose of antenatal betamethasone, and expedite caesarean section the same day without waiting for the second steroid dose
options.rationale: Progressive severe HELLP with rapidly falling platelets and rising transaminases mandates expedited delivery without completing the 48 hour steroid window. A single betamethasone dose still provides partial benefit. Caesarean is preferred over induction with unfavourable cervix. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Delivery timing in severe pre-eclampsia and HELLP under 34 weeks balances neonatal benefits of antenatal corticosteroids against rapid maternal deterioration. Stable disease can be temporised for 48 hours to complete betamethasone, but progressive HELLP (falling platelets, rising transaminases, neurological symptoms, oliguria, abruption, eclampsia, or non-reassuring foetal status) mandates expedited delivery the same day regardless of steroid status. Caesarean is preferred when the cervix is unfavourable.
key_points: Severe HELLP under 34 weeks: try to give betamethasone but do not delay delivery if progressive
Indications for immediate delivery: uncontrolled BP, eclampsia, abruption, falling platelets, rising ALT/LDH, non-reassuring CTG
Single dose of betamethasone provides partial neonatal benefit
Routine platelet transfusion only if < 20 or bleeding
Caesarean preferred when cervix unfavourable
explanation.pearls: Progressive HELLP with platelets falling more than 50 percent in 24 hours mandates same-day delivery: the maternal mortality curve outpaces the steroid window benefit, and a single betamethasone dose still reduces neonatal respiratory distress syndrome.

stem:
A 35-year-old man with bipolar I disorder has been on lithium carbonate 1000 mg daily for eight years with steady-state levels around 0.7 mmol/L on annual monitoring. His general practitioner prescribed ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily two weeks ago for new shoulder pain.

He now presents to a metropolitan emergency department with three days of coarse tremor, persistent nausea and vomiting, unsteady gait, and his partner reports he has become mildly confused over the last 24 hours. He has had no falls. He takes no other medications and has not started any new herbal supplements. He has continued his lithium tablets through the illness. He has no chronic kidney disease history and his last creatinine three months ago was 78 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 92, blood pressure 134/82, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: serum lithium 1.8 mmol/L (0.6-0.8), sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 122 micromol/L (60-110), eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m squared, ECG sinus rhythm with flattened T waves

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Stop lithium and ibuprofen, commence intravenous 0.9 percent saline for volume expansion, and contact the renal team to arrange urgent haemodialysis
options.rationale: Lithium level 1.8 with neurological features and impaired renal function meets criteria for moderate-to-severe lithium toxicity. NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance through prostaglandin-mediated vasoconstriction. Haemodialysis is indicated for level greater than 4 mmol/L, or 2.5 to 4 with severe symptoms or renal failure, or 1.5 to 2.5 with significant neurological involvement. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Continue lithium at the current dose, switch ibuprofen to paracetamol 1 g four times daily, and recheck the lithium level in 24 hours at clinic review
options.rationale: Continuing lithium at this level with neurological involvement risks deterioration including seizures, arrhythmias, and irreversible neurological injury. Immediate cessation is required. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Reduce lithium dose to 500 mg daily, continue ibuprofen at half dose with a proton pump inhibitor for gastric protection
options.rationale: Dose reduction without cessation does not address acute toxicity, and continued NSAID exposure will perpetuate the renal clearance impairment. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Stop lithium for 48 hours then restart at 500 mg, switch mood stabiliser to sodium valproate, and continue ibuprofen as prescribed by the general practitioner
options.rationale: Premature restart before level clears risks rebound toxicity. Switching to valproate is a longer-term consideration rather than acute management of a toxic level. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Switch lithium to a sustained release preparation and add ondansetron 8 mg three times daily for vomiting and supportive oral rehydration solution
options.rationale: Sustained-release lithium does not reduce toxicity; it changes the kinetics. The acute step is to remove lithium, not to reformulate it. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

explanation.summary: Lithium toxicity is precipitated commonly by dehydration, NSAID use (reduced renal prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation), ACE inhibitor or thiazide initiation, and acute kidney injury. Management is immediate cessation of lithium and precipitating drug, intravenous saline for volume expansion, and consideration of haemodialysis for severe toxicity (level above 4, or 2.5 to 4 with severe symptoms or renal failure, or 1.5 to 2.5 with significant neurological signs). Coarse tremor and ataxia indicate moderate toxicity.
key_points: Common precipitants: NSAID, ACE inhibitor, thiazide, dehydration, AKI
Symptoms: coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, vomiting, dysarthria, seizures (severe)
Haemodialysis if level > 4, or 2.5-4 with severe symptoms or renal failure
Stop lithium plus precipitating drug, give intravenous saline
Long-term follow-up: thyroid, parathyroid, and renal function monitoring
explanation.pearls: Ibuprofen reduces lithium clearance by around 25 percent through prostaglandin-mediated renal vasoconstriction and can push a previously therapeutic patient into toxicity within days; routine paracetamol is the preferred analgesic on lithium.

stem:
A 28-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been taking clozapine for four weeks after two previous trials of risperidone and olanzapine with persistent psychotic symptoms. He is now on clozapine 250 mg daily with significant reduction in auditory hallucinations and improved engagement at his community clinic. He has no fever, no sore throat, no rash, and no recent infection.

He has had weekly full blood counts since starting clozapine through the registry-monitored program. His latest result today shows white cells 4.2 and absolute neutrophil count 2.0. He is asking the consultant psychiatrist what monitoring schedule applies for the next year and how it relates to his current results. He has no other medical comorbidities and takes no other medications. He understands the requirement for ongoing blood tests and is happy to comply with the schedule.

data_table.Investigations: white cells 4.2 x10^9/L (4-11), absolute neutrophil count 2.0 x10^9/L (2.0-7.5), haemoglobin 142 g/L (130-180), platelets 230 x10^9/L (150-400), CRP 3 mg/L (<5), trough clozapine level 380 micrograms/L (350-600 therapeutic)

options.text: Increase to twice weekly full blood count for the next four weeks given the borderline neutrophil count and continue treatment unchanged
options.rationale: Counts of 2.0 are at the lower limit of the green zone and do not trigger increased monitoring frequency. Twice weekly is not a routine clozapine monitoring interval. (Source: TGA Clozapine Risk Management Plan)

options.text: Continue weekly full blood count until 18 weeks of clozapine, then four-weekly indefinitely while the patient remains on clozapine
options.rationale: The Australian clozapine registry protocol requires weekly haematological monitoring for the first 18 weeks (highest risk period for agranulocytosis), then four-weekly thereafter for the duration of therapy. (Source: TGA Clozapine Risk Management Plan)

options.text: Switch to monthly full blood count from week five given stable counts and good clinical response with no adverse effects in the first month
options.rationale: Monthly monitoring is not appropriate before 18 weeks because the highest risk of agranulocytosis is within the first 18 weeks of treatment. (Source: TGA Clozapine Risk Management Plan)

options.text: Stop clozapine immediately and switch to oral olanzapine 20 mg at night because the absolute neutrophil count is at the lower limit of normal
options.rationale: Absolute neutrophil count of 2.0 is at the lower border of the green zone and does not require cessation. The red zone (cease clozapine and admit) is below 1.5. (Source: TGA Clozapine Risk Management Plan)

options.text: Continue weekly full blood count indefinitely as a routine policy for all patients prescribed clozapine throughout their treatment course
options.rationale: Weekly indefinite monitoring is not the registry protocol and over-burdens the patient. Frequency steps down after 18 weeks of stable counts. (Source: TGA Clozapine Risk Management Plan)

explanation.summary: Clozapine is regulated through a national TGA risk management plan because of the risk of agranulocytosis and other haematological events. Australian protocol requires weekly full blood count for the first 18 weeks of treatment (highest risk window), then four-weekly indefinitely while the patient remains on clozapine. Trigger thresholds use the absolute neutrophil count: green zone (>= 2.0) continue, amber (1.5-1.99) twice weekly, red (< 1.5) cease and admit.
key_points: Weekly FBC for first 18 weeks of clozapine
Then four-weekly indefinitely
Green: ANC >= 2.0; amber: 1.5-1.99; red: < 1.5
Red zone: cease clozapine, admit, haematology referral
Also monitor for myocarditis (first 4 weeks): troponin, CRP, ECG
explanation.pearls: Agranulocytosis risk on clozapine peaks within the first 18 weeks (around 0.8 percent cumulative incidence) then falls; this is why the Australian registry steps weekly monitoring down to four-weekly only after week 18.

stem:
A 35-year-old man with major depressive disorder has been taking paroxetine 40 mg daily for two years. He stopped the medication five days ago without consulting his general practitioner because he felt better and thought he no longer needed it. His mood has been stable for the past six months on the medication.

He now presents to his general practitioner with five days of dizziness on standing, sensations he describes as electric brain shocks, persistent nausea, agitation, and difficulty sleeping. He denies any suicidal ideation and his mood is currently good. He has had no fever, no abdominal pain, and no new medications. He does not use alcohol or recreational drugs. His blood pressure and other observations are normal. He is keen to know what his treating doctor recommends he do next.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 84, blood pressure 122/76, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure him that discontinuation symptoms typically resolve spontaneously within 24 hours and continue total cessation with general supportive care
options.rationale: Untreated paroxetine discontinuation syndrome usually persists for one to three weeks (paroxetine has a short half-life and high discontinuation rate). Reassurance alone is inadequate. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 20 mg daily as a bridging therapy for one week and stop the fluoxetine completely without further taper
options.rationale: Bridging with fluoxetine is sometimes used for severe discontinuation syndrome but seven days is too short, increases fluoxetine accumulation, and does not address the underlying need for a gradual taper. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg daily as an alternative serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor to provide ongoing maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Switching antidepressant class is not the answer to discontinuation symptoms and venlafaxine has a similar or worse discontinuation profile than paroxetine. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Restart paroxetine 40 mg daily and then taper by twenty five percent of the dose every two to four weeks over several months
options.rationale: The recommended approach is to restart at the original dose and then taper slowly (typically 25 percent reduction every 2 to 4 weeks) over months. Paroxetine has the worst discontinuation profile of common SSRIs and requires the slowest taper. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Add lorazepam 1 mg three times daily for symptomatic management of agitation and insomnia and continue paroxetine cessation
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines do not treat SSRI discontinuation syndrome and risk dependence in this 35 year old. The underlying need is to restart paroxetine and taper. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: SSRI discontinuation syndrome presents with FINISH features (Flu-like symptoms, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbances including brain zaps, Hyperarousal). It is most common with short half-life agents (paroxetine, venlafaxine) and is differentiated from depressive relapse by onset within days, sensory symptoms, and rapid response to reinstatement. Management is restart at the original dose and taper gradually (25 percent reduction every 2 to 4 weeks) over months.
key_points: FINISH mnemonic for discontinuation symptoms
Highest risk: paroxetine, venlafaxine (short half-lives)
Onset within days of stopping; resolves within hours of reinstatement
Slow taper: 25 percent reduction every 2-4 weeks for at-risk agents
Fluoxetine has long half-life and rarely causes discontinuation syndrome
explanation.pearls: Paroxetine's discontinuation syndrome is the worst of the SSRIs because of its short half-life and lack of active metabolites; tapering by 5 to 10 mg every 2 to 4 weeks is appropriate.
